questions.text: Which of the following best describes a "static website" in the context of Web 1.0?

options.text: A website that loads very slowly due to large images
A website where every visitor receives the exact same HTML content, which only changes when the developer manually updates the file
A website that doesn't use JavaScript or CSS for styling
A website that works without an internet connection

questions.explanation: Explanation: A static website serves the exact same HTML file to every visitor, regardless of who they are, what time it is, or what they've done before. The content changes only when the owner edits the HTML file and re-uploads it (via FTP). There is no database, no personalization, no user accounts. Option A confuses loading speed with static/dynamic nature. Option C is wrong - static sites can have CSS and basic JavaScript. Option D describes offline/cached apps. The critical defining feature is: identical content for all visitors, manually updated by the developer.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: A static website serves the exact same HTML file to every visitor, regardless of who they are, what time it is, or what they've done before. The content changes only when the owner edits the HTML file and re-uploads it (via FTP). There is no database, no personalization, no user accounts. Option A confuses loading speed with static/dynamic nature. Option C is wrong - static sites can have CSS and basic JavaScript. Option D describes offline/cached apps. The critical defining feature is: identical content for all visitors, manually updated by the developer.

questions.text: Why is HTML described as a "markup language" rather than a "programming language"?

options.text: Because HTML was invented before programming languages existed
Because HTML runs slower than programming languages like Python or Java
Because HTML only describes the structure and appearance of content using tags, but has no logic - no conditions, no loops, no functions
Because HTML can only be read by browsers, not by servers

questions.explanation: Explanation: The defining characteristic of a programming language is that it can express logic - decisions (if/else), repetition (loops), and reusable functions. HTML does none of these. It simply annotates text with tags: <h1> means "this is a main heading," <p> means "this is a paragraph," <img> means "display an image." HTML cannot say "if the user is from Mumbai, show biryani recipes." It can only describe what content looks like, not how to compute it. This distinction is crucial for blockchain developers - Solidity IS a programming language with full logic, conditions, and functions. HTML is just description.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: The defining characteristic of a programming language is that it can express logic - decisions (if/else), repetition (loops), and reusable functions. HTML does none of these. It simply annotates text with tags: <h1> means "this is a main heading," <p> means "this is a paragraph," <img> means "display an image." HTML cannot say "if the user is from Mumbai, show biryani recipes." It can only describe what content looks like, not how to compute it. This distinction is crucial for blockchain developers - Solidity IS a programming language with full logic, conditions, and functions. HTML is just description.

questions.text: GeoCities was shut down by Yahoo in 2009, deleting 38 million personal websites. Which of the following blockchain concepts directly addresses this problem?

options.text: Proof of Stake consensus - validators ensure websites are always available
Gas fees - users pay to store their website permanently
Decentralized storage (IPFS, Arweave) - content stored across distributed nodes worldwide, no single company can delete it
Smart contracts - automated agreements that prevent companies from shutting down

questions.explanation: Explanation: GeoCities' fatal flaw was that user content was stored on Yahoo's centralized servers. Yahoo decided to delete them - users had no recourse. Decentralized storage protocols like IPFS (InterPlanetary File System) and Arweave store content across thousands of nodes worldwide. To delete content from IPFS, you'd need to simultaneously remove it from every single node - computationally and practically impossible if the network is large enough. MST Blockchain integrates with such decentralized storage, enabling permanent, censorship-resistant content hosting. Option A (consensus) relates to transaction validation, not storage. Option D (smart contracts) can't force a company to keep servers running.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: GeoCities' fatal flaw was that user content was stored on Yahoo's centralized servers. Yahoo decided to delete them - users had no recourse. Decentralized storage protocols like IPFS (InterPlanetary File System) and Arweave store content across thousands of nodes worldwide. To delete content from IPFS, you'd need to simultaneously remove it from every single node - computationally and practically impossible if the network is large enough. MST Blockchain integrates with such decentralized storage, enabling permanent, censorship-resistant content hosting. Option A (consensus) relates to transaction validation, not storage. Option D (smart contracts) can't force a company to keep servers running.

questions.text: In what way was Web 1.0 actually MORE decentralized than Web 2.0?

options.text: Web 1.0 used peer-to-peer protocols, while Web 2.0 uses client-server models
In Web 1.0, anyone could run their own server and publish freely; Web 2.0 funneled everyone onto a few dominant platforms (Facebook, YouTube, Instagram) that control publishing
Web 1.0 had no central servers at all; Web 2.0 introduced centralized data centers
Web 1.0 was decentralized in ownership because users owned their browsers

questions.explanation: Explanation: This is a nuanced but critical question. In Web 1.0, publishing was distributed - anyone with a web server could publish anything to anyone in the world without asking permission from any platform. Thousands of independent websites competed. In Web 2.0, paradoxically, content creation became MORE centralized: instead of owning your own server, you publish through Facebook, YouTube, or Instagram - who then control your audience, your monetization, and your content's visibility. If Instagram bans you, your audience is gone. This is why the blockchain/Web3 movement is described as "a return to Web 1.0's openness, with Web 2.0's interactivity, plus real ownership." Option A is incorrect - both eras use client-server. Option C is false - Web 1.0 had servers, just many distributed ones.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: This is a nuanced but critical question. In Web 1.0, publishing was distributed - anyone with a web server could publish anything to anyone in the world without asking permission from any platform. Thousands of independent websites competed. In Web 2.0, paradoxically, content creation became MORE centralized: instead of owning your own server, you publish through Facebook, YouTube, or Instagram - who then control your audience, your monetization, and your content's visibility. If Instagram bans you, your audience is gone. This is why the blockchain/Web3 movement is described as "a return to Web 1.0's openness, with Web 2.0's interactivity, plus real ownership." Option A is incorrect - both eras use client-server. Option C is false - Web 1.0 had servers, just many distributed ones.

questions.text: Statement: "Web 1.0 was inferior to Web 2.0 in every way - it had no advantages whatsoever, and its replacement by Web 2.0 was an entirely positive development for users."
questions.explanation: Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):While Web 2.0 brought genuine improvements - interactivity, ease of publishing, richer experiences, social connection - it also introduced serious new problems that Web 1.0 did not have. First, Web 1.0 collected essentially no user data, while Web 2.0 platforms track every click, build behavioral profiles, and sell them to advertisers - a significant privacy regression. Second, Web 1.0 was genuinely decentralized in publishing: anyone with a server could publish freely. Web 2.0 funneled billions of people into platforms controlled by a handful of corporations (Meta, Google, Amazon), giving these companies unprecedented censorship power. Third, Web 1.0 creators owned their content outright - when GeoCities shut down, it was tragic, but it was also possible to run your own server and not be dependent on any platform. Web 2.0 creators are entirely dependent on platform algorithms, policies, and goodwill for their income and reach. The transition was a trade of openness and privacy for convenience and interactivity - not a purely positive development.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Verdict: FALSE

Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):

While Web 2.0 brought genuine improvements - interactivity, ease of publishing, richer experiences, social connection - it also introduced serious new problems that Web 1.0 did not have. First, Web 1.0 collected essentially no user data, while Web 2.0 platforms track every click, build behavioral profiles, and sell them to advertisers - a significant privacy regression. Second, Web 1.0 was genuinely decentralized in publishing: anyone with a server could publish freely. Web 2.0 funneled billions of people into platforms controlled by a handful of corporations (Meta, Google, Amazon), giving these companies unprecedented censorship power. Third, Web 1.0 creators owned their content outright - when GeoCities shut down, it was tragic, but it was also possible to run your own server and not be dependent on any platform. Web 2.0 creators are entirely dependent on platform algorithms, policies, and goodwill for their income and reach. The transition was a trade of openness and privacy for convenience and interactivity - not a purely positive development.

1 Mark: States FALSE and identifies at least one genuine advantage of Web 1.0 that Web 2.0 eliminated (privacy, decentralized publishing, or content ownership)
+1 Mark: Provides a nuanced comparison covering both the improvements of Web 2.0 AND the specific regressions, showing balanced understanding
